title: Service detail | Piel en Armonia
description: Dermatology route files with clinical criteria, clear stages, and measurable follow-up.
voiceNote: Each route explains risk, options, and next step in clear clinical language so decisions feel confident and informed.

thesis.body: Each stage shows what to watch, how to decide, and when to escalate to protect progression.

statement.alt: Clinical direction at Piel en Armonia

ui.internalMessageFallback: Your route is confirmed in consultation with clear clinical explanation.
ui.faqAnswerNote: Guidance answer; your final plan is confirmed in consultation.

bookingStatus.title: Online booking under maintenance
bookingStatus.description: Web scheduling returns soon. Until then, you can begin through telemedicine.

services.title: Comprehensive diagnosis
services.summary: Full skin, hair, and nail reading to define priorities from the first consultation.
services.lead: The first consultation organizes your case: what needs action now, what can wait, and how progress will be measured.
faq: What should I bring to the visit?
Do I need tests before attending?
faqAnswers: Bring your current medications, allergy history, previous treatments, and recent photos. That baseline lets us decide faster and with better context.
Only when they add a concrete decision point. If needed, tests are requested after the initial clinical evaluation.
First follow-up is commonly set between weeks 3 and 6, or sooner if pain, bleeding, or rapid worsening appears.

services.title: Acne and rosacea
services.summary: Control of inflammatory flares and sensitivity with realistic medical goals.
services.lead: We aim for calmer skin and fewer relapses through timely adjustments, not abrupt plan changes.
faq: What to do during an intense relapse?
faqAnswers: Most patients see less inflammation between weeks 4 and 8 when the routine is followed consistently.
Yes, once the skin barrier is stable and phototype evaluation supports safe parameter selection.
Pause irritants, keep gentle cleansing, and request medical adjustment within 48 to 72 hours.

services.title: Warts
services.summary: Management of common, plantar, and periungual warts by type and location.
services.lead: Technique is selected by wart type and location so treatment is effective without harming healthy skin.
faqAnswers: Many warts respond in 1 to 3 sessions, while deeper plantar lesions may need extra controls.
Short discomfort can occur. We adjust technique and timing to keep treatment controlled and tolerable.
Recurrence is possible, but risk falls when follow-up is completed and hygiene measures are maintained.

services.title: Bumps on arms and legs
services.summary: Route for rough texture and persistent follicular changes.
services.lead: Barrier repair and follicular control are combined to improve texture in a stable, realistic way.
faq: How long does texture improvement take?
faqAnswers: Keratosis pilaris is common, but we also evaluate eczema, folliculitis, and other inflammatory causes.
With daily consistency, texture often improves between week 6 and week 10.
Avoid harsh exfoliants, strong fragrance products, and drying cleansers that disrupt barrier balance.

services.title: Scars
services.summary: Combined strategy to improve contour, tone, and texture by scar type.
services.lead: Real scar improvement is layered: contour, tone, and texture are treated in a clear sequence.
faq: Do all scars respond the same?
How many sessions are usually required?
faqAnswers: No. Response changes with scar type, lesion age, phototype, and adherence to the planned sequence.
Plans often include 3 to 6 sessions, with reassessment at every stage to adjust intensity.
Daily photoprotection, barrier hydration, and friction control are essential to protect improvement.

services.title: Skin cancer
services.summary: Suspicious lesion assessment for early risk detection.
services.lead: Warning signs are prioritized early so we decide quickly between close follow-up and referral.
faq: How often should moles be checked?
Can this route start through telemedicine?
faqAnswers: In higher-risk profiles, checks are usually every 6 to 12 months, then personalized by findings.
Seek prompt review for asymmetry, irregular borders, fast color change, or non-healing bleeding lesions.
Yes for first triage. If risk signs are detected, we prioritize in-clinic evaluation without delay.

services.title: Chemical peel
services.summary: Skin renewal for tone, texture, and selected pigmentation concerns.
services.lead: Peel intensity is calibrated to renew skin safely while respecting your phototype and daily rhythm.
faq: How many recovery days are common?
faqAnswers: Recovery usually lasts 3 to 7 days depending on peel depth and individual sensitivity.
Yes, but only in medically sequenced timing windows that prevent cumulative irritation on sensitive skin.
Pregnancy, active irritation, poor sun-protection adherence, or high sensitivity can delay indication.

services.title: Mesotherapy
services.summary: Micro-interventions for deep hydration and skin quality.
services.lead: Short sessions plus close follow-up are integrated to improve hydration, glow, and skin quality.
faq: Which routes combine best with it?
faqAnswers: Mild redness may appear for 24 to 48 hours, then most patients return to routine.
A common plan is 3 to 5 sessions spaced every 3 to 4 weeks.
It often pairs well with medical hydration, gentle peels, or biostimulators once skin is stable.

services.title: Dermatology laser
services.summary: Laser treatments with traceable clinical parameters and post-session follow-up.
services.lead: Every laser parameter is chosen by clinical objective and skin response, never by generic templates.
faq: Is it suitable for all phototypes?
faqAnswers: Yes, with parameters adjusted by phototype and goal to reduce post-inflammatory pigmentation risk.
It varies by indication: pigment, vascular concerns, texture, scars, or medical hair reduction.
Strict photoprotection and early control are mandatory; first review is often set in 2 to 4 weeks.

services.title: Medical botox
services.summary: Expression-line management with natural look and responsible dosing.
services.lead: Precise dosing softens expression lines while preserving natural movement and facial identity.
faq: How often is it usually repeated?
What care should be maintained after treatment?
faqAnswers: Initial softening appears around days 3 to 5, with full effect commonly seen by days 10 to 14.
Average duration is 3 to 4 months, varying with metabolism, area, and dosing strategy.
Avoid intense massage and strenuous training for 24 hours, and follow all aftercare instructions.

services.title: Collagen biostimulators
services.summary: Progressive stimulation for support and firmness in mild to moderate structural loss.
services.lead: Zone-based planning stimulates collagen progressively and keeps outcomes coherent over time for each treated area.
faq: How long until changes are visible?
How many sessions are commonly suggested?
Can this combine with medical botox?
faqAnswers: Change is progressive, often starting around week 6 and consolidating over 3 to 6 months.
Most plans use 2 or 3 sessions, defined by laxity level, treated zones, and tissue response.
Yes, it combines well within an integrated facial plan with clear timing and supervision.

services.title: Skin, hair and nails
services.summary: Cross-route care for combined, chronic, or variable progression symptoms.
services.lead: When multiple symptoms overlap, we coordinate one integrated reading instead of treating isolated pieces.
faq: How often should follow-up be scheduled?
faqAnswers: This route includes mixed cases: hair shedding, nail changes, and skin symptoms appearing together.
Yes. Telemedicine works as first triage and defines when direct in-clinic examination is needed.
First follow-up is commonly planned in 4 to 8 weeks based on findings and early response.

services.title: Pediatric dermatology
services.summary: Dermatology care for children and adolescents with clear family communication.
services.lead: Medical decisions are translated into simple steps for home, school, and family routine.
faq: What should we bring to the first visit?
How is the plan explained to children and adolescents?
faqAnswers: Bring a symptom timeline, previous treatments, allergy history, and recent photos to speed clinical reading.
We explain the plan in plain language with practical steps for home, school, and daily routine.
Yes, it works well for interim questions and follow-up when no warning signs are present.
